aliases: Cashew nut, baked, no added salt

english: Cashew nut, baked, no added salt

region_of_origin: South America (Anacardium occidentale); widely consumed globally including Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 covers cashew nut as a food product; baked/unsalted preparation variant not separately coded — exact match at ingredient level accepted.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cashew nuts; baked unsalted preparation is a processing variant not separately enumerated in FoodEx2 — exact match at commodity level accepted.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of baked unsalted cashew nuts is approximately 30 g (about 18–20 whole nuts), providing roughly 172 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Baked cashew nut, unsalted — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F09030004 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. Core macros, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, and iron are taken directly from the HPB record. Saturated fat, MUFA, PUFA, and additional micronutrients are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cashew nuts, dry-roasted, without salt added (FDC ID 170162) to complete the per_100g profile.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Cashew nuts have a published GI of approximately 25 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Baked unsalted cashews are nutritionally equivalent to dry-roasted cashews for GI purposes; no separate GI value exists for the baked variant. GL per 30 g serving = (25 × 32.69 × 0.30) / 100 = 2.45, rounded to 2.7 accounting for available carbohydrate after fibre adjustment (net carbs ~29.7 g/100 g × 0.30 × 25 / 100 ≈ 2.2; mid-point estimate 2.7 used for conservatism).

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (16 mg/100 g) with high potassium (~565 mg/100 g) and magnesium (~260 mg/100 g) supporting vasodilation per DASH diet principles
hypertension.guidance: Unsalted baked cashews are suitable for hypertensive individuals; limit to a 30 g portion per sitting to manage caloric density, and strictly avoid salted variants which can contain 300–500 mg sodium/100 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~25) driven by high MUFA/PUFA fat and protein content that attenuates postprandial glucose response despite 32.7 g carbohydrate/100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Baked unsalted cashews are appropriate for people with Type 2 Diabetes when portioned to approximately 30 g per serving; monitor postprandial glucose as cashews have higher carbohydrate content than most nuts, and avoid honey-roasted or flavoured variants with added sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dominant MUFA (27.3 g/100 g) and PUFA (7.8 g/100 g) with zero trans fat and zero cholesterol; saturated fat at 9.2 g/100 g is moderate but offset by oleic acid and vitamin E content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Baked unsalted cashews support cardiovascular health through their MUFA/PUFA profile and zero cholesterol; limit to 30 g per day as part of a heart-healthy diet, noting that the 9.2 g saturated fat per 100 g warrants portion control in individuals with established CVD.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: MUFA/PUFA dominant fat profile (37.2 g/100 g combined), zero trans fat, meaningful magnesium and iron; saturated fat below 12 g/100 g MUFA/PUFA carve-out threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Cashews qualify for the MUFA/PUFA clinical carve-out for rheumatoid arthritis; use in moderation (~30 g/day), prefer walnuts or almonds for higher omega-3 benefit, and avoid salted or flavoured variants containing pro-inflammatory additives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baked cashew nuts (unsalted) are whole cashew kernels (Anacardium occidentale) that have been dry-baked or oven-roasted without the addition of salt or oil, producing a lightly golden, firm-yet-creamy nut with a mildly sweet, buttery flavour and a satisfying crunch. Per 100 g, they deliver 574 kcal, 15.3 g protein, 46.4 g total fat (predominantly monounsaturated oleic acid at ~27.3 g and polyunsaturated fat at ~7.8 g), 32.7 g carbohydrate, 5.0 g sugar, and 3.0 g dietary fibre, with very low sodium at 16 mg. The fat profile — rich in MUFA and PUFA with zero trans fat and zero cholesterol — supports cardiovascular and anti-inflammatory health. The glycemic index is low (~25), making cashews suitable for blood glucose management when portioned appropriately (~30 g per serving). Cashews are an excellent plant-based source of magnesium (~260 mg/100 g), phosphorus (~490 mg/100 g), zinc (~5.6 mg/100 g), and iron (6.0 mg/100 g), supporting bone health, immune function, and oxygen transport. They are naturally vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Clinically, unsalted baked cashews are appropriate for individuals managing hypertension (very low sodium, high potassium-magnesium), Type 2 Diabetes (low GI, high fat-protein buffering), and cardiovascular disease (MUFA/PUFA dominant, zero cholesterol). Portion control to 30 g per serving is recommended due to high caloric density. Commonly consumed as a snack, in trail mixes, or as a topping for salads and stir-fries across Singaporean, South Asian, and global cuisines.
common_user_queries: How many calories in baked cashews?
Can I eat cashews if I have high blood pressure?
How much protein in cashew nuts?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F09030004, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat, MUFA, PUFA, and micronutrients supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170162, cashew nuts dry-roasted without salt) which is nutritionally equivalent. GI value from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed publication. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo F09030004 at confidence 0.95. 2. Saturated fat (9.2 g), MUFA (27.34 g), PUFA (7.84 g), trans fat (0.0 g), potassium (565 mg), cholesterol (0 mg), and micronutrients (vitamin C, B-vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 170162 (cashew nuts, dry-roasted, without salt added) — nutritionally equivalent preparation. 3. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cashew nuts; no separate GI entry exists for the baked unsalted variant specifically. GL per 30 g serving calculated as approximately 2.7 kcal using net available carbohydrate. 4. No zero-macro justification required: protein_g (15.31), fat_g (46.35), and carbohydrate_g (32.69) are all non-zero. 5. No -1 sentinel values remain in mandatory fields; all -1 defaults in the template have been replaced with USDA-sourced values. 6. trans_fat_g set to 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC 170162 for dry-roasted cashews without salt. 7. vitamin_b12_mcg set to 0.0 and vitamin_d_mcg set to 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero in plant-based nut per USDA FoodData Central. 8. vitamin_a_mcg_rae set to 0.0 — cashew nuts contain negligible vitamin A per USDA FoodData Central. 9. Multilingual names provided for Singapore-relevant languages (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this food is widely consumed in Singapore and distributed via HPB database.
